sourceMovie.title: Kartavya
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Kartavya (2026) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Pulkit's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Murder, My Sweet
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The unease that lingers after the credits that Kartavya delivers so well reappears in Murder, My Sweet (1944), anchored by the same crime and drama instincts. Different film, same essential itch scratched.

recommendations.title: Mulholland Drive
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kartavya uses drama and thriller to get under your skin; Mulholland Drive (2001) works the same seam with different tools. The momentum and clean stakes is the reward both films share.

recommendations.title: The Thomas Crown Affair
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kartavya uses crime and drama to get under your skin; The Thomas Crown Affair (1999) works the same seam with different tools. The emotional honesty that stings a little is the reward both films share.

recommendations.title: Bullitt
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The unease that lingers after the credits that makes Kartavya linger is the through-line here. Bullitt (1968) gets there through crime and thriller—same destination, entirely different route.

recommendations.title: The Big Sleep
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Big Sleep (1946) understands that crime and thriller works best when it trusts the audience—the same principle that drives Kartavya. Both films close on unease that lingers after the credits you won't shake quickly.

recommendations.title: The Dead Pool
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kartavya fans keep landing on The Dead Pool (1988) because the crime and thriller wavelength is unmistakable. Both films build toward unease that lingers after the credits without cheating to get there.

recommendations.title: French Connection II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kartavya fans keep landing on French Connection II (1975) because the crime and thriller wavelength is unmistakable. Both films build toward unease that lingers after the credits without cheating to get there.

recommendations.title: Die Hard: With a Vengeance
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Viewers who connected with Kartavya's thriller core tend to find Die Hard: With a Vengeance (1995) almost immediately. The momentum and clean stakes is recognisable even when the characters and plot are entirely new.

recommendations.title: Live Free or Die Hard
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kartavya fans keep landing on Live Free or Die Hard (2007) because the thriller wavelength is unmistakable. Both films build toward momentum and clean stakes without cheating to get there.

recommendations.title: Secret Window
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The momentum and clean stakes that Kartavya delivers so well reappears in Secret Window (2004), anchored by the same thriller instincts. Different film, same essential itch scratched.

faq.question: What should I watch after Kartavya?
faq.answer: Start with Murder, My Sweet—it's the closest match—then work through My Sweet and Mulholland Drive and The Thomas Crown Affair for the wider orbit.

faq.question: What genre is Kartavya?
faq.answer: Kartavya (2026) is best described as Crime, Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Kartavya worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.0/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
